title: Vision Transformer
definition: A transformer architecture applied directly to images by splitting them into fixed-size patches (16x16 or 32x32 pixels), flattening them into sequences, and treating each patch as a 'token'. Self-attention across patches replaces convolutions.
why_used: When trained on large datasets, ViT matches or outperforms CNNs like ResNet. Its attention mechanism captures global relationships between image regions from the start, unlike CNNs which build global context layer by layer.
interview_point: ViT's key advantage over CNNs: self-attention can capture long-range dependencies between distant image patches in a single layer. CNNs need many layers to achieve global context. Limitation: ViT needs much more data (JFT-300M) than CNNs — it doesn't have the inductive bias (translation invariance, locality) that CNNs exploit.
advantages: Scales better than CNNs with more data
limitations: Requires large amounts of data to outperform CNNs
Higher computational cost for small images
real_world_example:
```python
import torchvision.models as models
import torch.nn as nn

# Load pretrained ViT-B/16
model = models.vit_b_16(weights='IMAGENET1K_V1')

# Fine-tune for custom classification
num_features = model.heads.head.in_features
model.heads.head = nn.Linear(num_features, num_classes)

# ViT expects 224x224 images normalized with ImageNet stats
from torchvision import transforms
preprocess = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.Resize(256),
    transforms.CenterCrop(224),
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                         std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
])
```

title: SegFormer
definition: A transformer-based semantic segmentation model that combines a hierarchical transformer encoder with a lightweight MLP decoder. Avoids positional encodings (scale-agnostic) and uses overlapping patch merging to capture local continuity.
why_used: State-of-the-art semantic segmentation with simpler architecture than previous models. Efficient at multiple scales (B0-B5 variants) and outperforms CNN-based segmentation on major benchmarks.
interview_point: SegFormer's key innovations: 1) Mix Transformer (MiT) encoder with overlapping patch merging — captures local continuity between patches. 2) No positional encodings — uses 2D average pooling to handle any resolution without retraining. 3) Lightweight all-MLP decoder — fuses multi-scale features simply but effectively.
limitations: Less interpretable than U-Net skip connections
real_world_example:
```python
from transformers import SegformerForSemanticSegmentation, SegformerImageProcessor
from PIL import Image
import torch

processor = SegformerImageProcessor.from_pretrained('nvidia/segformer-b2-finetuned-cityscapes-1024-1024')
model = SegformerForSemanticSegmentation.from_pretrained('nvidia/segformer-b2-finetuned-cityscapes-1024-1024')

# Inference
image = Image.open('street.jpg')
inputs = processor(images=image, return_tensors='pt')

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# Get segmentation map
logits = outputs.logits  # Shape: (batch_size, num_labels, H/4, W/4)
predicted_map = logits.argmax(dim=1).squeeze().numpy()
```

title: EasyOCR
definition: A Python library for Optical Character Recognition (OCR) supporting 80+ languages. Uses a two-stage pipeline: CRAFT (Character Region Awareness for Text detection) for text detection + CRNN (Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network) for text recognition.
why_used: Simplest OCR solution with a single pip install and 2-line inference. Supports multilingual text, handwriting, and natural scene text out of the box.
interview_point: EasyOCR pipeline: 1) Detection: CRAFT network finds text regions as bounding boxes. 2) Recognition: CRNN (CNN features + BiLSTM + CTC loss) reads the text in each bounding box. CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) loss is used because the output length varies from input length.
limitations: Slower than commercial OCR (Google Vision API)
real_world_example:
```python
import easyocr
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np

# Initialize reader (downloads models on first run)
reader = easyocr.Reader(
    ['en', 'hi'],   # Languages to detect
    gpu=True        # Use GPU if available
)

# Read text from image
results = reader.readtext(
    'document.jpg',
    detail=1,       # Return bounding boxes and confidence
    paragraph=True  # Merge text into paragraphs
)

# Each result: (bbox, text, confidence)
for bbox, text, confidence in results:
    print(f'Text: "{text}" | Confidence: {confidence:.3f}')

# Quick single-line extraction
texts = reader.readtext('image.jpg', detail=0)
full_text = ' '.join(texts)
```

title: DeepLabV3+
definition: A semantic segmentation CNN by Google that uses atrous (dilated) convolutions to capture multi-scale context without losing resolution. DeepLabV3+ adds an encoder-decoder structure with skip connections for sharper boundaries.
why_used: State-of-the-art semantic segmentation with excellent boundary detail. Atrous convolutions efficiently enlarge the receptive field without increasing parameters or reducing resolution.
interview_point: Atrous (dilated) convolutions: insert zeros (holes) between convolution kernel elements to increase receptive field without increasing parameters. Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) applies multiple atrous convolutions with different rates to capture multi-scale context in parallel.
advantages: Strong performance on standard benchmarks (PASCAL VOC, Cityscapes)
limitations: Computationally heavier than simpler segmentation models
Requires labeled pixel-level masks for training
real_world_example:
```python
import torch
from torchvision.models.segmentation import deeplabv3_resnet101

# Load pretrained DeepLabV3
model = deeplabv3_resnet101(weights='DEFAULT')
model.eval()

# Preprocess image
from torchvision import transforms
transform = transforms.Compose([
    transforms.ToTensor(),
    transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                         std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225])
])

image_tensor = transform(image).unsqueeze(0)

with torch.no_grad():
    output = model(image_tensor)['out']

# Get segmentation map
seg_map = output.argmax(dim=1).squeeze().numpy()
```
